# Competitive landscape: where Movemental’s comparisons are fair, fuzzy, or fixable

**Series:** Build research — exploratory articles  
**Companion raw file:** `docs/build/research/raw/12-competitive-landscape-raw.md`  
**Date:** 2026-04-13  
**Prompt:** `docs/build/research/prompts/12-competitive-landscape.md`

---

## Why this document exists

Movemental’s sales narrative bundles four competitor archetypes—**traditional publishers**, **creator platforms**, **site builders**, and **custom agencies**—plus a **venture-builder** identity (`research-048`) and a **technical proof** bundle (`C-PR04`). Purchasing decisions hinge on those contrasts. This article answers one question bluntly: **which comparisons survive contact with public pricing, export policies, and publishing-industry mechanics—and which need rewriting before they become liabilities.**

---

## Traditional publishing: speed and economics are real; the villain story is oversimplified

### Timelines

Third-party publishing educators—not Movemental competitors, but credible clocks—describe a long arc. **Greenleaf Book Group** states that **traditional** publishing, including agent acquisition and seasonal list constraints, can run **two to three years** end-to-end, with **production after contract** often **nine months to two years** and **about eighteen months average** once a manuscript enters the machine. **Hybrid** publishing is positioned between trad and self-pub: commonly **six to twelve months**, with **about nine months** typical for a quality hybrid run.

Against that backdrop, a **twelve-week onboarding** claim (from Movemental positioning elsewhere) is a **different axis**: onboarding is not the same as “book in Barnes & Noble.” Movemental wins honestly when it says **time-to-ship digital surface**—not when it implies parity with **full trade production + retail sell-in** unless it actually delivers those functions.

### “Publishers take 85–90%”

Trade authors often receive on the order of **roughly ten to fifteen percent of list price** on hardcovers (format- and deal-dependent); ebooks frequently use **percent of net** with different bands. So Movemental’s **“publisher takes 85–90%”** can be defended as **shorthand for the author’s royalty slice on print list price**.

The ethical problem is tonal: it sounds like **publisher profit**. In reality, **retailers, returns, printing, warehousing, marketing, and the advance gamble** eat much of the difference. A sophisticated author will hear “85–90%” and mentally translate it to **“you’re exaggerating.”** Better copy: **“Authors commonly keep a low double-digit royalty on print list; the rest funds the machine you may not need for your digital-first thesis.”**

### What publishers still buy you

Movemental should never argue as if publishers provide **only** gatekeeping. They provide **capital** (advances), **editorial bench**, **design and production**, **returns handling**, **sales relationships**, and **imprint imprimatur**. If Movemental is not offering advance financing and retail distribution, **say so plainly**—then the comparison is **fair** as a **scope trade**, not a **superiority dunk**.

### Hybrids are the silent competitor

**Greenleaf**, **Author Solutions–style** packages, and a long tail of **paid publishing services** occupy the same emotional territory as Movemental for authors who want **professional polish faster than New York**. If the deck only contrasts Random House vs. Movemental, **savvy buyers** will ask, “Why not hybrid?” Movemental needs a **named hybrid column** in competitive materials—not deep trash talk, but **clear differentiation on ownership, data, AI posture, and ongoing product economics.**

---

## Substack, Patreon, Ghost, Kajabi, Teachable, Mighty Networks: ownership is not what the slogan says

### The Substack “rental” claim

Internal messaging sometimes implies creators **do not own their audience** on Substack. **Legally and practically, that is a weak phrasing.** Substack’s own help center documents **CSV export** of subscribers (including plan types and engagement columns). Patreon likewise documents **CSV export** paths for creator relationship data.

The **fair** critique of Substack is not “you lack the email list.” It is **platform dependency**: **payment rails**, **iOS in-app purchase surcharges**, **algorithmic discovery**, **reader habit** (“I read you inside Substack”), **feature roadmap risk**, and **incomplete migration** of the full engagement graph beyond what CSV captures. Substack also charges a **straight ten percent** platform fee on paid subscriptions (plus Stripe costs; Apple can stack on mobile). That fee is **regressive in success**—at scale, it rivals SaaS bills.

Movemental should **retire** “you don’t own your audience” unless counsel blesses a narrow definition. Replace with: **“You can export emails and still be structurally dependent on their checkout, discovery, and mobile tax architecture.”**

### Ghost, Beehiiv, Kit (ConvertKit), Kajabi, Teachable, Mighty Networks

These products **partition the problem differently**:

- **Ghost(Pro)** advertises **zero percent** platform transaction fees on memberships at common tiers, with **member import/export** on paid plans—strong **“keep the margin”** positioning versus Substack.  
- **Kajabi** bundles courses, funnels, email—**high SaaS price**, **low take-rate narrative** (verify current **January 2026** pricing refresh on their official page).  
- **Teachable** separates **platform transaction fees** (notably on lower tiers) from payment processing—creators must model **both**.  
- **Mighty Networks** bundles **community + courses + apps** with **plan-dependent transaction fees**—useful comparator when Movemental sells **cohort + community**.  
- **Beehiiv / Kit** emphasize **newsletter growth + automation** and migration playbooks—Movemental should know **who wins on pure newsletter economics**.

None of these automatically delivers Movemental’s **multi-tenant “movement OS” thesis**—but they **do** deliver **ship speed** and **known price curves**. Respect them.

---

## Squarespace and WordPress: the comparison must be segmented

### Site builders

**Squarespace** (verify live pricing) remains the correct comparator for **“beautiful site in a weekend.”** It is not a fair comparator for **RLS-scoped multi-tenant Postgres, agent pipelines, and bespoke formation analytics** unless Movemental explicitly limits the claim to **marketing site parity**.

### WordPress + plugins

A serious thought-leader stack can approximate many features: **LearnDash** or **LifterLMS** for courses, **BuddyBoss** for community, **WooCommerce** for money, dozens of integrations for email. **Gap:** engineering to reach **tenant isolation, auditability, and AI orchestration** at Movemental’s intended depth is **non-trivial**—not impossible, but **not a plugin toggle**.

### Custom agency costs

Industry explainers (agency blogs—**not peer-reviewed**) commonly band **marketing WordPress** builds in the **single-digit to mid five figures**, while **enterprise** stacks with heavy integrations drift toward **six figures**. Movemental’s **“$100K+ and six to twelve months”** claim is **realistic for the top decile of scope** (custom design system, LMS, community, commerce, integrations, compliance)—and **misleading as a universal stick** against “any WordPress.”

**Recommendation:** use **two baselines**: **$8K–$25K marketing site** vs **$75K–$150K+ movement platform** when arguing against bespoke builds.

### “Network effects”

WordPress does not ship **cross-creator discovery** by default. Movemental’s honest differentiator is **graph + shared rails + (maybe) marketplace dynamics**—not “WordPress lacks MySQL.” Name the **user-visible network primitive** (endorsement, co-teaching, shared corpus, federation) or the claim reads as buzzword.

---

## Venture-builder studio: inspiring metaphor, easy to challenge literally

Public **venture studios** (e.g., **Atomic**, **Hexa / eFounders**) co-found repeated startups, supply **capital and shared services**, and take **material equity** across a portfolio. That is not automatically what Movemental does when partnering with a **specific movement leader**.

**Aligned incentives** (`research-048`) is strongest when expressed as **revenue share or success-based pricing** with transparent caps—weak when it sounds like **equity co-founding** without cap table reality.

**Safer positioning:** **“Productized venture partnership”** or **“studio-style build + operate”** unless Movemental truly spins out **independent portfolio companies** the way Atomic does.

---

## Technical proof (`C-PR04`): impressive story with three audit flags

### Table count

Argument collateral cites **105+** tables in one breath and **168+** in another. That inconsistency will erode trust faster than any competitor review. **Pick one audited number** tied to a **schema export** or migration file, or stop citing tables publicly.

In **this** repository snapshot, `src/lib/db/schema.ts` is a **placeholder** with **one** table definition. Any public “105+” claim must clarify **which codebase / database** it refers to.

### “Sixty percent faster than industry standard”

Internal docs assert **sixty percent faster page loads** without (in the excerpts reviewed) attaching **Lighthouse? CrUX? which percentile? which URLs? which competitor set?** Until published, treat as **marketing**—not proof.

### “One developer in six months”

Buyers will hear **velocity**; engineers hear **bus factor**. The literature and common sense agree: **single-threaded delivery** is a **risk surface**. Movemental should pair the brag with **continuity story** (documentation, backups, runbooks, team depth)—otherwise sophisticated customers will mark it **yellow flag**, not green.

### Are many tables “meaningful”?

**Only** if they map to **domain boundaries, security isolation, and test coverage**. Otherwise it is **complexity theater**. Prefer metrics buyers understand: **uptime**, **time-to-first-value**, **support SLAs**, **data residency**, **export guarantees**.

---

## Faith-sector platforms

**Subsplash**, **Faithlife**, **RightNow Media**, and denominational **LMS** rails optimize for **churches and institutions**. They can be expensive, quote-driven, and **feature-rich for operations**. They are not always optimized for **individual scenius** or **AI-forward author workflows**. Compare **use-case fit**, not “secular bad / sacred good.”

A denominational executive buying **RightNow** is solving **licensed curriculum at scale** for congregations. A movement leader building **personal intellectual property**, **cohort courses**, and **public argument** is solving a **creator-brand + pedagogy + community** problem. The overlap is “video in a portal,” but the **go-to-market, permissions model, and editorial voice** differ enough that Movemental should **map personas** before claiming replacement.

---

## Patreon vs Substack vs “owned” email (nuance buyers expect)

**Patreon** and **Substack** both sit on top of **someone else’s member ledger**, **chargeback policies**, and **feature roadmaps**. Patreon’s creator tooling emphasizes **membership tiers and benefits**; Substack emphasizes **writing + discovery + simplicity**. Neither gives you **root on the database** or **portable application logic**—if Movemental’s value is **custom data models** (formation pathways, assessments, agent traces, multi-tenant isolation), that is a **legitimate** wedge even when CSVs exist.

Still, Movemental should assume buyers have read **Ghost’s pricing page**: **zero percent** platform fees on memberships at common hosted tiers, plus **import/export** language in the feature grid. Ghost self-hosting adds DevOps burden but strengthens the **“I can walk away”** story. Movemental wins against Ghost when the buyer **does not want to run Kubernetes**—not when pretending Ghost lacks export.

**Beehiiv** and **Kit** deserve mention because they attack Substack’s **success tax** with **flat SaaS + growth tooling**. A spreadsheet-minded author will model ten percent of **$30k MRR** and ask why Movemental beats **Beehiiv + Webflow + Circle**. Have the answer ready in **total cost of ownership** (engineering, integrations, support hours), not in **table counts**.

---

## WordPress parity: what plugins can and cannot buy you

LearnDash-style stacks can ship **quizzes, drip content, certificates, and basic cohort flows** quickly. BuddyBoss-style stacks can ship **groups, forums-ish activity, and member profiles**. Where stacks typically strain is the **same product** operating as **multi-tenant SaaS** with **row-level security guarantees**, **audit logs**, **agent tool permissions**, and **predictable upgrade paths** across dozens of plugins. Agencies can absolutely build that—it is mostly a **budget and maintenance** question.

Movemental’s fairest claim is not “WordPress cannot.” It is: **“An opinionated product reduces integration risk and keeps the graph coherent.”** That is a **risk-management** sale to buyers who have already been burned by **plugin conflicts**, **security patches**, and **contractor turnover**.

---

## “Aligned incentives” — what would misalignment look like?

Even with revenue share, incentives drift when:

- Movemental optimizes for **template throughput** while the leader needs **bespoke theology and voice**.  
- The leader wants **traffic growth hacks** while Movemental optimizes for **trust and formation quality** (see EEAT research elsewhere in this series).  
- Success fees push **short-term launches** over **long-term content stewardship**.

Movemental should publish **what happens after year two**: pricing escalators, IP assignment, export guarantees, and **off-boarding**. Studios that only tell the **honeymoon story** lose sophisticated buyers.

---

## Honest positioning matrix (summary)

| Alternative | Movemental wins when… | Movemental loses when… |
|-------------|----------------------|-------------------------|
| **Trade publisher** | Speed, digital ownership, always-on iteration, AI augmentation policy | Advance money, retail distribution, imprimatur, returns machine |
| **Hybrid publisher** | Software + AI + ongoing product, not one-off book package | Purely price-shopping production quality |
| **Substack** | Deeper product surface, lower take at scale hypothesis, custom workflows | Simple newsletter + fastest time to first dollar |
| **Ghost** | Margin retention + self-host path + developer aesthetics | Zero engineering tolerance |
| **Kajabi / Teachable / Mighty** | Bespoke movement graph + multi-tenant thesis + services wrap | DIY operator who loves no-code tools |
| **WordPress stack** | Integrated governance + speed of opinionated product | Tinkerer with low budget and time |
| **Custom agency** | Predictable productized outcome + long operate partnership | Unique creative vision requiring artisan agency |

---

## Recommendations before the comparisons go live

1. **Rewrite publisher economics** with **royalty vocabulary**, not “publisher keeps profit.”  
2. **Replace audience-ownership slogans** with **dependency mechanics** (payments, discovery, mobile tax, CSV limits).  
3. **Add hybrid publishing** as a first-class competitor column.  
4. **Segment WordPress cost** into **marketing** vs **platform-class** builds.  
5. **Reconcile C-PR04 numbers** or remove table-count bragging.  
6. **Publish a methodology footnote** for performance claims—or delete them from “proof” tier.  
7. **Soften venture-studio** language unless the cap table matches Atomic-style studios.

---

## Closing

Movemental’s strongest competitive story is not “everyone else is incompetent.” It is **scope selection**: **digital-first movement leaders** who need **integrated web + learning + community + AI** with **credible trust surfaces**, delivered **faster than trade publishing cycles** and **more coherently than a bag of WordPress plugins**—with economics that **do not tax success** the way ten-percent-in-perpetuity platforms do at scale.

That story is **good enough to win** without overstating the villains—because **buyers who recognize the oversell** are exactly the partners Movemental says it wants.
